question_text: A particle is dropped from a height H. The de Broglie wavelength of the particle as a function of height is proportional to
question_html: A particle is dropped from a height H. The de Broglie wavelength of the particle as a function of height is proportional to

options.explanation: The velocity of freely falling body after falling from a height {tex}H=v=\sqrt{2 g H}{/tex} We know that de Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{m v}=\frac{h}{m \sqrt{2 g H}}{/tex} h, m and g are constant {tex}\therefore \frac{h}{m \sqrt{2 g}}{/tex} is constant {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{H}} \Rightarrow \lambda \propto H^{\frac{-1}{2}}{/tex} , this is the required result.

question_text: Consider a beam of electrons (each electron with energy E 0 ) incident on a metal surface kept in an evacuated chamber. Then
question_html: Consider a beam of electrons (each electron with energy E0) incident on a metal surface kept in an evacuated chamber. Then

options.text: no electrons will be emitted as only photons can emit electrons.
options.html: no electrons will be emitted as only photons can emit electrons.

options.text: electrons can be emitted but all with an energy, E 0 .
options.html: electrons can be emitted but all with an energy, E0.

options.text: electrons can be emitted with any energy, with a maximum of {tex}E_0 - \phi{/tex} ( {tex}\phi{/tex} is the work function)
options.html: electrons can be emitted with any energy, with a maximum of {tex}E_0 - \phi{/tex} ({tex}\phi{/tex} is the work function)

options.text: electrons can be emitted with any energy, with a maximum of E 0 .
options.html: electrons can be emitted with any energy, with a maximum of E0.
options.explanation: When a beam of electrons of energy E 0 of each electron incident on a metal surface kept in a vacuum, then due to elastic collisions with electrons on surface, the energy of incident electrons will be transferred to the emitted electrons. To emit the electrons below the surface a part of E 0 of incident electrons is consumed against work function so the energy of emitted electrons becomes less than E 0 . So, the maximum energy of emitted electrons can be E 0 and with any energy less than E 0 , when part of the incident energy of the electron is used in liberating the electrons from the surface of the metal.

correct_option_text: electrons can be emitted with any energy, with a maximum of E 0 .

question_html: An electron is moving with an initial velocity {tex}v=v_{0} \hat{i}{/tex} and is in a magnetic field {tex}B=B_{0} \hat{\jmath}{/tex}. Then it’s de Broglie wavelength

options.explanation: Given, {tex}\vec{v}=v_{0} \hat{i}{/tex} and {tex}B=B_{0} \hat{j}{/tex} The magnetic force on moving electron is given as, {tex}F=-e(\bar{v}\times\vec{B}){/tex} {tex}=-e\left[v_{0}\hat{i} \times B_{0}\hat{j}\right]{/tex} {tex}=-e v_{0} B_{0} \hat{i} \times \hat{j}{/tex} {tex}F=-e v_{0} B_{0} \hat{k}{/tex} So the force is perpendicular to v and B both as the force is perpendicular to the velocity. So will not change v or mv so the de-Broglie wavelength remains same.

question_text: Relativistic corrections become necessary when the expression for the kinetic energy {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} , becomes comparable with mc 2 , where m is the mass of the particle. At what de Broglie wavelength will relativistic corrections become important for an electron? {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 10 nm {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 10 -1 nm {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 10 -4 nm {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 10 -6 nm
question_html:
Relativistic corrections become necessary when the expression for the kinetic energy {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex}, becomes comparable with mc2, where m is the mass of the particle. At what de Broglie wavelength will relativistic corrections become important for an electron?

  1. {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 10 nm
  2. {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 10-1nm
  3. {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 10-4nm
  4. {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 10-6nm
solution_text: (c) and (d) are correct. {tex}v=\frac{h}{m \lambda}\left(\begin{array}{l} h=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \\ m=9 \times 10-31 \mathrm{Kg} \end{array}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{9 \times 10^{-31} \lambda}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34+31}}{9 \lambda}=\frac{0.73 \times 10^{-3}}{\lambda}{/tex} For option {tex}\lambda=10 \mathrm{nm}=10 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}=10^{-8} \mathrm{m}{/tex} {tex}\therefore v=7.3 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{+8}=7.3 \times 10^{4}<3 \times 10^{8}{/tex} (speed of light) {tex}\lambda=10^{-1} \mathrm{nm}=10^{-1} \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}=10^{-10} \mathrm{m}{/tex} {tex}\therefore v=\frac{7.3 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-10}}=7.3 \times 10^{-4+10}{/tex} {tex}=7.3 \times 10^{6} \approx 10^{7}<10^{8}{/tex} (Speed of light) {tex}\lambda=10^{-4} \mathrm{nm}=10^{-4} \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}=10^{-13} \mathrm{m}{/tex} {tex}v=\frac{7.3 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-13}}=7.3 \times 10^{-4+13}{/tex} {tex}=7.3 \times 10^{9} \approx 10^{10}>10^{8}{/tex} (speed of light) {tex}\lambda=10^{-6} \mathrm{nm}=10^{-15} \mathrm{m}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{v}=7.3 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{15}=7.3 \times 10^{11} \approx 10^{12}>10^{8}{/tex} (Speed of light) So the velocity of the electron is more for option (c) and (d) where the relativistic correction becomes necessary although the speed of electron is 7.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m/s is comparable with (c) speed of light, there must be a relativistic correction. Options are (c) and (d).
solution_html:
(c) and (d) are correct.

{tex}v=\frac{h}{m \lambda}\left(\begin{array}{l} h=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \\ m=9 \times 10-31 \mathrm{Kg} \end{array}\right){/tex}
{tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{9 \times 10^{-31} \lambda}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34+31}}{9 \lambda}=\frac{0.73 \times 10^{-3}}{\lambda}{/tex}
For option

  1. {tex}\lambda=10 \mathrm{nm}=10 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}=10^{-8} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore v=7.3 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{+8}=7.3 \times 10^{4}<3 \times 10^{8}{/tex} (speed of light)
  2. {tex}\lambda=10^{-1} \mathrm{nm}=10^{-1} \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}=10^{-10} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore v=\frac{7.3 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-10}}=7.3 \times 10^{-4+10}{/tex} {tex}=7.3 \times 10^{6} \approx 10^{7}<10^{8}{/tex} (Speed of light)
  3. {tex}\lambda=10^{-4} \mathrm{nm}=10^{-4} \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{m}=10^{-13} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
    {tex}v=\frac{7.3 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-13}}=7.3 \times 10^{-4+13}{/tex} {tex}=7.3 \times 10^{9} \approx 10^{10}>10^{8}{/tex} (speed of light)
  4. {tex}\lambda=10^{-6} \mathrm{nm}=10^{-15} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{v}=7.3 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{15}=7.3 \times 10^{11} \approx 10^{12}>10^{8}{/tex} (Speed of light)

So the velocity of the electron is more for option (c) and (d) where the relativistic correction becomes necessary although the speed of electron is 7.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m/s is comparable with (c) speed of light, there must be a relativistic correction. Options are (c) and (d).

question_text: Two particles A 1 and A 2 of masses m 1 , m 2 (m 1 > m 2 ) have the same de Broglie wavelength. Then their momenta are the same. their energies are the same. energy of A 1 is less than the energy of A 2 . energy of A 1 is more than the energy of A 2 .
question_html:
Two particles A1 and A2 of masses m1, m2 (m1 > m2) have the same de Broglie wavelength. Then

  1. their momenta are the same.
  2. their energies are the same.
  3. energy of A1 is less than the energy of A2.
  4. energy of A1 is more than the energy of A2.
solution_text: (a) and (c) are correct. {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} or {tex}p \propto \frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}\frac{p_{1}}{p_{2}}=\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\because \lambda_{1}=\lambda_{2}=\lambda{/tex} (given) P 1 = P 2 verifies {tex}E_{a}=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{m^{2} v^{2}}{m}=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}{/tex} {tex}E \propto \frac{1}{m}{/tex} [as p 1 = p 2 = cos. (as proved above)] {tex}\frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}}=\frac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}}<1{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} m 1 > m 2 (given)] {tex}\therefore{/tex} E 2 > E 1 verfies answer (c)
solution_html:
(a) and (c) are correct.
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex}
{tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} or {tex}p \propto \frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{p_{1}}{p_{2}}=\frac{\lambda_{2}}{\lambda_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\because \lambda_{1}=\lambda_{2}=\lambda{/tex} (given)
P1 = P2 verifies
{tex}E_{a}=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{m^{2} v^{2}}{m}=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}{/tex}
{tex}E \propto \frac{1}{m}{/tex} [as p1 = p2 = cos. (as proved above)]
{tex}\frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}}=\frac{m_{2}}{m_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{E_{1}}{E_{2}}<1{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} m1 > m2 (given)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} E2 > E1 verfies answer (c)

question_text: The de Broglie wavelength of a photon is twice the de Broglie wavelength of an electron. The speed of the electron is {tex}v_{e}=\frac{c}{100}{/tex} . Then {tex}\frac{E_{c}}{E_{p}}=10^{-4}{/tex} {tex}\frac{E_{e}}{E_{p}}=10^{-2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{p_{e}}{m_{e} c}=10^{-2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{p_{e}}{m_{e} c}=10^{-4}{/tex}
question_html:
The de Broglie wavelength of a photon is twice the de Broglie wavelength of an electron. The speed of the electron is {tex}v_{e}=\frac{c}{100}{/tex}. Then

  1. {tex}\frac{E_{c}}{E_{p}}=10^{-4}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{E_{e}}{E_{p}}=10^{-2}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\frac{p_{e}}{m_{e} c}=10^{-2}{/tex}
  4. {tex}\frac{p_{e}}{m_{e} c}=10^{-4}{/tex}
solution_text: (b) and (c) are correct. For electron, {tex}\lambda_{e}=\frac{h}{m_{e} v_{e}}=\frac{h}{m_{e}(c / 100)}=\frac{100 h}{m_{e} c}{/tex} ...(i) Kinetic energy, {tex}E_{e}=\frac{1}{2} m_{e} v_{e}^{2}{/tex} or {tex}E_{e}=\frac{1}{2} m_{e} v_{e}^{2}{/tex} ...(ii) For photon of wavelength {tex}\lambda_{p}{/tex} , energy {tex}E_{p}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{p}}=\frac{h c}{2 \lambda_{e}}\left(\because \lambda_{p}=2 \lambda_{0}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{E_{p}}{E_{e}}=\frac{h c}{2 \lambda_{e}} \times \frac{2 \lambda_{e}^{2} m_{e}}{h^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\lambda_{e} m_{e} c}{h}=\frac{100 h}{m_{e} c} \times \frac{m_{e} c}{h}=100{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{E_{e}}{E_{p}}=\frac{1}{100}=10^{-2}{/tex} For electron, move P e m e v e = {tex}m_e \times \frac {c}{100}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{p_{e}}{m_{e} c}=\frac{1}{100}=10^{-2}{/tex}
solution_html:
(b) and (c) are correct.
For electron, {tex}\lambda_{e}=\frac{h}{m_{e} v_{e}}=\frac{h}{m_{e}(c / 100)}=\frac{100 h}{m_{e} c}{/tex} ...(i)
Kinetic energy, {tex}E_{e}=\frac{1}{2} m_{e} v_{e}^{2}{/tex} or {tex}E_{e}=\frac{1}{2} m_{e} v_{e}^{2}{/tex} ...(ii)
For photon of wavelength {tex}\lambda_{p}{/tex}, energy {tex}E_{p}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{p}}=\frac{h c}{2 \lambda_{e}}\left(\because \lambda_{p}=2 \lambda_{0}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{E_{p}}{E_{e}}=\frac{h c}{2 \lambda_{e}} \times \frac{2 \lambda_{e}^{2} m_{e}}{h^{2}}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{\lambda_{e} m_{e} c}{h}=\frac{100 h}{m_{e} c} \times \frac{m_{e} c}{h}=100{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \frac{E_{e}}{E_{p}}=\frac{1}{100}=10^{-2}{/tex}
For electron, move Pe meve = {tex}m_e \times \frac {c}{100}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{p_{e}}{m_{e} c}=\frac{1}{100}=10^{-2}{/tex}

question_html:
Photons absorbed in the matter are converted to heat. A source emitting n photon/sec of frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} is used to convert 1kg of ice at 0°C to water at 0°C. Then, the time T took for the conversion

  1. decreases with increasing n, with {tex}\nu{/tex} fixed.
  2. decreases with n fixed, {tex}\nu{/tex} increasing.
  3. remains constant with n and {tex}\nu{/tex} changing such that n{tex}\nu{/tex} = constant.
  4. increases when the product n{tex}\nu{/tex} increases.
solution_html:
(a), (b) and (c) are correct.
Energy spent to convert ice into water
= mL = (1000g) {tex}\times{/tex} 80 cal g-1 = 80000 cal
The energy of photons used {tex}=n T \times E=n T \times h \nu{/tex}
{tex}\therefore n T H \nu=m L{/tex} or {tex}T=\frac{m L}{n H \nu}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore T \propto \frac{1}{\nu}{/tex} when n fixed.
Thus T is constant if {tex}\nu{/tex} is constant.

question_text: A particle moves in a closed orbit around the origin, due to a force which is directed towards the origin. The de Broglie wavelength of the particle varies cyclically between two values {tex}\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}{/tex} with {tex}\lambda_{1}>\lambda_{2}{/tex} . Which of the following statement are true? The particle could be moving in a circular orbit with origin as centre The particle could be moving in an elliptic orbit with origin as its focus. When the de Broglie wavelength is {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex} , the particle is nearer the origin than when its value is {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex} . When the de Broglie wavelength is {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex} , the particle is nearer the origin than when its value is {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex}
question_html:
A particle moves in a closed orbit around the origin, due to a force which is directed towards the origin. The de Broglie wavelength of the particle varies cyclically between two values {tex}\lambda_{1}, \lambda_{2}{/tex} with {tex}\lambda_{1}>\lambda_{2}{/tex}. Which of the following statement are true?

  1. The particle could be moving in a circular orbit with origin as centre
  2. The particle could be moving in an elliptic orbit with origin as its focus.
  3. When the de Broglie wavelength is {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex}, the particle is nearer the origin than when its value is {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex}.
  4. When the de Broglie wavelength is {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex}, the particle is nearer the origin than when its value is {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex}
solution_text: (b) and (d) are correct. The de-Broglie wavelength of the particle can be varying cyclically between two values {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex} , if a particle is moving in an elliptical orbit with origin as its one focus. Let v 1 , v 2 be the speed of the particle at A and B respectively and origin is at focus O. If {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex} , {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex} are the de Broglie wavelength associated with particle while moving at A and B respectively, then {tex}\lambda_{1}=\frac{h}{m v_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_{2}=\frac{h}{m v_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\frac{v_{2}}{v_{1}},{/tex} since {tex}\lambda_{1}>\lambda_{2} \therefore v_{2}>v_{1}{/tex} From figure, we note that origin O is close to P than A. thus options (b) and (d) are true.
solution_html:
(b) and (d) are correct.

The de-Broglie wavelength of the particle can be varying cyclically between two values {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex}, if a particle is moving in an elliptical orbit with origin as its one focus. Let v1, v2 be the speed of the particle at A and B respectively and origin is at focus O. If {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex}, {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex} are the de Broglie wavelength associated with particle while moving at A and B respectively, then
{tex}\lambda_{1}=\frac{h}{m v_{1}}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_{2}=\frac{h}{m v_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{2}}=\frac{v_{2}}{v_{1}},{/tex} since {tex}\lambda_{1}>\lambda_{2} \therefore v_{2}>v_{1}{/tex}
From figure, we note that origin O is close to P than A. thus options (b) and (d) are true.

question_text: A proton and an {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle are accelerated the same potential difference. How are the {tex}{\lambda _p}{/tex} and {tex}{\lambda_\alpha }{/tex} related to each other?
question_html: A proton and an {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle are accelerated the same potential difference. How are the {tex}{\lambda _p}{/tex} and {tex}{\lambda_\alpha }{/tex} related to each other?
solution_text: We know that, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{{\sqrt {2mqV} }}{/tex} Therefore, we have {tex}\frac{{{\lambda _p}}}{{{\lambda _\alpha }}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{\sqrt {{m_\alpha }{q_\alpha }} }}{{\sqrt {{m_p}{q_p}} }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{\sqrt {4{m_p} \times2 {q_p}} }}{{\sqrt {{m_p}{q _p}} }}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt 8{/tex}
solution_html:
We know that, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{{\sqrt {2mqV} }}{/tex}
Therefore, we have 
{tex}\frac{{{\lambda _p}}}{{{\lambda _\alpha }}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{\sqrt {{m_\alpha }{q_\alpha }} }}{{\sqrt {{m_p}{q_p}} }}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{{\sqrt {4{m_p} \times2 {q_p}} }}{{\sqrt {{m_p}{q _p}} }}{/tex} 
= {tex}\sqrt 8{/tex}

question_html:
1. In the explanation of the photoelectric effect, we assume one photon of frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} collides with an electron and transfers its energy. This leads to the equation for the maximum energy Emax of the emitted electron as
    {tex}E_{\max }=h \nu-\phi_{0}{/tex}
    here {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} is the work function of the metal. If an electron absorbs 2 photons (each of frequency {tex}\nu{/tex}) what will be the maximum energy for the emitted electron?
  2. Why is this fact (two photon absorption) not taken into consideration in our discussion of the stopping potential?
solution_html:
1. Here, 2 photons transfer its energy to one electron as E = h{tex}\nu{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{e}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{p}}{/tex}
    h{tex}\nu_e{/tex} = 2h{tex}\nu{/tex}
    {tex}\nu_{e}=2 \nu{/tex}
    The maximum energy of the emitted electron is given by ;-
    {tex}E_{\max }=h \nu_{e^{-}} \phi_{0}=h(2 \nu)-\phi_{0}=2 h \nu-\phi_{0}{/tex}
  2. The probability of absorbing 2 photons by an electron is very low due to their mass difference. So the possibilities of such emission of electrons are negligible.

question_text: There are materials which absorb photons of shorter wavelength and emit photons of longer wavelength. Can there be stable substances which absorb photons of larger wavelength and emit light of shorter wavelength?
question_html: There are materials which absorb photons of shorter wavelength and emit photons of longer wavelength. Can there be stable substances which absorb photons of larger wavelength and emit light of shorter wavelength?
solution_text: We know that as the wavelength of photon increases, its frequency decreases or energy increase. Case I: Photons of shorter wavelength i.e., of larger energy emits the photons of smaller energy here some energy, is consumed against work function. So, it is possible by law of conservation of energy. Case II: Photons of longer wavelength always emits photons of shorter wavelength and photons of smaller energy can never emit photons of larger energy as some part of energy (hv) is consumed in the work function {tex}(\phi){/tex} of the metal. Otherwise, it will discard the law of conservation of energy (universal law) so, it cannot be possible in stable materials.

Otherwise, it will discard the law of conservation of energy (universal law) so, it cannot be possible in stable materials.

question_text: Do all the electrons that absorb a photon comes out as photoelectrons?
question_html: Do all the electrons that absorb a photon comes out as photoelectrons?
solution_text: In photoelectric effect, we can observe that most of the electrons knocked by photons are scattered into the metal by absorbing photons. Only a few come out of the surface of metal whose energy becomes greater than the work function of the metal. So, not all the electrons that absorb a photon comes out from the metal surface.

So, not all the electrons that absorb a photon comes out from the metal surface.

question_text: There are two sources of light, each emitting a power of 100W. One emits X-rays of wavelength 1 nm and other visible light of at 500 nm. Find the ratio of number of photons of X-rays to the photons of visible light of the given wavelength?
question_html: There are two sources of light, each emitting a power of 100W. One emits X-rays of wavelength 1 nm and other visible light of at 500 nm. Find the ratio of number of photons of X-rays to the photons of visible light of the given wavelength?
solution_text: Given {tex}{{\lambda _1}}{/tex} = 1 nm, {tex}{{\lambda _2}}{/tex} = 500 nm Since the power is same, therefore both emit the same energy. Let n 1 and n 2 be the number of photons of X-rays and visible light. Let E 1 and E 2 be the energies emitted by one photon of each, then {tex}\ n_1 E_1{/tex} = {tex}\ n_2 E_2{/tex} {tex}{n_1}\frac{{hc}}{{{\lambda _1}}}{/tex} = {tex}{n_2}\frac{{hc}}{{{\lambda _2}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{n_1}}}{{{n_2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{\lambda _1}}}{{{\lambda _2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{{500}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given {tex}{{\lambda _1}}{/tex} = 1 nm, {tex}{{\lambda _2}}{/tex} = 500 nm
Since the power is same, therefore both emit the same energy.
Let n1 and n2 be the number of photons of X-rays and visible light. Let E1 and E2 be the energies emitted by one photon of each, then
{tex}\ n_1 E_1{/tex} = {tex}\ n_2 E_2{/tex}
{tex}{n_1}\frac{{hc}}{{{\lambda _1}}}{/tex} = {tex}{n_2}\frac{{hc}}{{{\lambda _2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{{n_1}}}{{{n_2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{\lambda _1}}}{{{\lambda _2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{{500}}{/tex}

question_text: Consider Fig. for photo-emission. How would you reconcile with momentum-conservation? Note light (photons) have momentum in a different direction than the emitted electrons.
question_html: Consider Fig. for photo-emission. How would you reconcile with momentum-conservation? Note light (photons) have momentum in a different direction than the emitted electrons.
solution_text: During photoelectric emission, the momentum of incident photon is transferred to the metal. At microscopic, level, atoms of a metal absorb the photon and its momentum is transferred mainly to the nucleus and electrons. The excited electron is emitted. Therefore, the conservation of momentum is to be considered as the momentum of incident photon transferred to the nucleus and electrons.
solution_html:
During photoelectric emission, the momentum of incident photon is transferred to the metal. At microscopic, level, atoms of a metal absorb the photon and its momentum is transferred mainly to the nucleus and electrons.
The excited electron is emitted. Therefore, the conservation of momentum is to be considered as the momentum of incident photon transferred to the nucleus and electrons.

question_text: Consider a metal exposed to the light of wavelength 600 nm. The maximum energy of the electron doubles when the light of wavelength 400 nm is used. Find the work function in eV.
question_html: Consider a metal exposed to the light of wavelength 600 nm. The maximum energy of the electron doubles when the light of wavelength 400 nm is used. Find the work function in eV.
solution_text: Let the maximum energies of emitted electrons are K 1 and K 2 when 600 nm and 400 nm visible light are used according to question K 2 = 2K 1 {tex}K_{\max }=h v-\phi=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-\phi{/tex} {tex}k_{1}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}-\phi{/tex} {tex}k_{2}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{2}}-\phi=2 K_{1}{/tex} {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_{2}}-\phi=2\left[\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}-\phi\right]=\frac{2 h c}{\lambda_{1}}-2 \phi{/tex} {tex}\phi=h c\left[\frac{2}{\lambda_{1}}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}\right]{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} hc = 1240 ev nm) {tex}\therefore \phi=1240\left[\frac{2}{600}-\frac{1}{400}\right] e V=\frac{1240}{200}\left[\frac{2}{3}-\frac{1}{2}\right]=6.2 \frac{(4-3)}{6}{/tex} Work function {tex}\phi=\frac{6.2}{6}=1.03{/tex} eV
solution_html:
Let the maximum energies of emitted electrons are K1 and K2 when 600 nm and 400 nm visible light are used according to question
K2 = 2K1
{tex}K_{\max }=h v-\phi=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-\phi{/tex}
{tex}k_{1}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}-\phi{/tex}
{tex}k_{2}=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{2}}-\phi=2 K_{1}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda_{2}}-\phi=2\left[\frac{h c}{\lambda_{1}}-\phi\right]=\frac{2 h c}{\lambda_{1}}-2 \phi{/tex}
{tex}\phi=h c\left[\frac{2}{\lambda_{1}}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}\right]{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} hc = 1240 ev nm)
{tex}\therefore \phi=1240\left[\frac{2}{600}-\frac{1}{400}\right] e V=\frac{1240}{200}\left[\frac{2}{3}-\frac{1}{2}\right]=6.2 \frac{(4-3)}{6}{/tex}
Work function {tex}\phi=\frac{6.2}{6}=1.03{/tex} eV

question_text: Assuming an electron is confined to a 1nm wide region, find the uncertainty in momentum using the Heisenberg Uncertainty principle ( {tex}\Delta{/tex} x {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} p {tex}\cong{/tex} h). You can assume the uncertainty in position {tex}\Delta{/tex} x as 1nm. Assuming p {tex}\approx{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex} p, find the energy of the electron in electron volts.
question_html: Assuming an electron is confined to a 1nm wide region, find the uncertainty in momentum using the Heisenberg Uncertainty principle ({tex}\Delta{/tex}x {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}p  {tex}\cong{/tex} h). You can assume the uncertainty in position {tex}\Delta{/tex}x as 1nm. Assuming p {tex}\approx{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}p, find the energy of the electron in electron volts.
solution_text: As electron revolves in circular path so {tex}\Delta{/tex} r = 1 nm =10 -9 m {tex}\Delta{/tex} x {tex}\times{/tex} Δp {tex}\cong{/tex} h [Given] {tex}\Delta p=\frac{h}{\Delta x}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \pi \Delta r}{/tex} JS {tex}\Delta p=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} Kg m/s {tex}=\frac{331}{314} \times 10^{-34+9}{/tex} or {tex}\Delta p=\frac{331}{314} \times 10^{-25}{/tex} {tex}\therefore E=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{m^{2} v^{2}}{2 m}=\frac{\Delta p^{2}}{2 m}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{331}{314} \times \frac{331}{314} \times \frac{10^{-25} \times 10^{25}}{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{331 \times 331 \times 10^{-50+31}}{314 \times 314 \times 18.2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{331 \times 331 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{314 \times 314 \times 18.2}{/tex} e {tex}=\frac{331 \times 331 \times 16}{314 \times 314 \times 182}=3.8 \times 10^{-2}{/tex} eV
solution_html:
As electron revolves in circular path so {tex}\Delta{/tex}r = 1 nm =10-9 m
{tex}\Delta{/tex}x {tex}\times{/tex} Δp {tex}\cong{/tex} h [Given]
{tex}\Delta p=\frac{h}{\Delta x}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \pi \Delta r}{/tex} JS
{tex}\Delta p=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} Kg m/s 
{tex}=\frac{331}{314} \times 10^{-34+9}{/tex} or {tex}\Delta p=\frac{331}{314} \times 10^{-25}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore E=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{m^{2} v^{2}}{2 m}=\frac{\Delta p^{2}}{2 m}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{331}{314} \times \frac{331}{314} \times \frac{10^{-25} \times 10^{25}}{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{331 \times 331 \times 10^{-50+31}}{314 \times 314 \times 18.2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{331 \times 331 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{314 \times 314 \times 18.2}{/tex}e
{tex}=\frac{331 \times 331 \times 16}{314 \times 314 \times 182}=3.8 \times 10^{-2}{/tex} eV

question_text: Two particles A and B of de-Broglie wavelength {tex}{\lambda_1}{/tex} and {tex}{\lambda_2}{/tex} combine to form a particle C. The process conserves momentum. Find the de-Broglie wavelength of the particle C (the motion is one dimensional).
question_html: Two particles A and B of de-Broglie wavelength {tex}{\lambda_1}{/tex} and {tex}{\lambda_2}{/tex} combine to form a particle C. The process conserves momentum. Find the de-Broglie wavelength of the particle C (the motion is one dimensional).
solution_text: Since the motion is one dimensional, therefore, If the particles move in the same direction, momentum is given by ;- | {tex}\vec{p_C}{/tex} | = | {tex}\vec{p_A}{/tex} | + | {tex}\vec{p_B}{/tex} | {tex}\frac{h}{{{\lambda _C}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{{{\lambda _1}}}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{h}{{{\lambda _2}}}{/tex} or {tex}{{\lambda _C}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{\lambda _1}{\lambda _2}}}{{{\lambda _1} + {\lambda _2}}}{/tex} If the particles move in the opposite direction, momentum is given by :- | {tex}\vec{p_C}{/tex} | = | {tex}\vec{p_A}{/tex} | - | {tex}\vec{p_B}{/tex} | {tex}\frac{h}{{{\lambda _C}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{{{\lambda _1}}}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{h}{{{\lambda _2}}}{/tex} or {tex}{{\lambda _C}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{\lambda _1}{\lambda _2}}}{{{\lambda _1} - {\lambda _2}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Since the motion is one dimensional, therefore, 

  1. If the particles move in the same direction, momentum is given by ;-
    |{tex}\vec{p_C}{/tex}| = |{tex}\vec{p_A}{/tex}| + |{tex}\vec{p_B}{/tex}|
    {tex}\frac{h}{{{\lambda _C}}}{/tex}={tex}\frac{h}{{{\lambda _1}}}{/tex} + {tex}\frac{h}{{{\lambda _2}}}{/tex} 
    or {tex}{{\lambda _C}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{\lambda _1}{\lambda _2}}}{{{\lambda _1} + {\lambda _2}}}{/tex}
  2. If the particles move in the opposite direction, momentum is given by :-
    |{tex}\vec{p_C}{/tex}| = |{tex}\vec{p_A}{/tex}| - |{tex}\vec{p_B}{/tex}|
    {tex}\frac{h}{{{\lambda _C}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{{{\lambda _1}}}{/tex} - {tex}\frac{h}{{{\lambda _2}}}{/tex} 
    or {tex}{{\lambda _C}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{\lambda _1}{\lambda _2}}}{{{\lambda _1} - {\lambda _2}}}{/tex}

question_text: A neutron beam of energy E scatters from atoms on a surface with a spacing d = 0.1nm. The first maximum of intensity in the reflected beam occurs at {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o . What is the kinetic energy E of the beam in eV?
question_html: A neutron beam of energy E scatters from atoms on a surface with a spacing d = 0.1nm. The first maximum of intensity in the reflected beam occurs at {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30o. What is the kinetic energy E of the beam in eV?
solution_text: By Bragg’s law of diffraction, condition for nth maxima is {tex}2 d \sin \theta=n \lambda{/tex} n = 1 so {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 2dsin {tex}\theta{/tex} [ {tex}\theta{/tex} = 30 o (Given)] = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 sin 30 o ( {tex}\because{/tex} d = 0.1 nm) p = {tex}\frac{h}{\lambda}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{10^{-10}}=6.6 \times 10^{-2.4}{/tex} kg m/s {tex}E=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{m^{2} v^{2}}{m}=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}{/tex} {tex}\therefore E=\frac{6.6 \times 6.6 \times 10^{-24} \times 10^{24}}{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-27}}{/tex} J {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 6.6 \times 10^{-48}}{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-27} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} e V=\frac{66 \times 66 \times 10^{-18+46}}{2 \times 16 \times 16}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{33 \times 33}{126} \times 10^{-2}{/tex} = 8.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 = 0.085 eV
solution_html:
By Bragg’s law of diffraction, condition for nth maxima is
{tex}2 d \sin \theta=n \lambda{/tex}
n = 1 so {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 2dsin{tex}\theta{/tex} [{tex}\theta{/tex} = 30o (Given)]
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 sin 30o ({tex}\because{/tex} d = 0.1 nm)
p = {tex}\frac{h}{\lambda}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{10^{-10}}=6.6 \times 10^{-2.4}{/tex} kg m/s
{tex}E=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{m^{2} v^{2}}{m}=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore E=\frac{6.6 \times 6.6 \times 10^{-24} \times 10^{24}}{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-27}}{/tex} J
{tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 6.6 \times 10^{-48}}{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-27} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} e V=\frac{66 \times 66 \times 10^{-18+46}}{2 \times 16 \times 16}{/tex}
{tex}E=\frac{33 \times 33}{126} \times 10^{-2}{/tex} = 8.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 = 0.085 eV

question_text: Consider a thin target (10 -2 m square, 10 -3 m thickness) of sodium, which produces a photocurrent of 100 {tex}\mu{/tex} A when a light of intensity 100W/m 2 ( {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 660nm) falls on it. Find the probability that a photoelectron is produced when a photon strikes a sodium atom. [Take density of Na = 0.97 kg/m 3 ].
question_html: Consider a thin target (10-2m square, 10-3m thickness) of sodium, which produces a photocurrent of 100{tex}\mu{/tex}A when a light of intensity 100W/m2 ({tex}\lambda{/tex} = 660nm) falls on it. Find the probability that a photoelectron is produced when a photon strikes a sodium atom. [Take density of Na = 0.97 kg/m3].
solution_text: {tex}6 \times 10^{26}{/tex} Na atoms weights 23 kg. Volume of target = (10 -4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 ) = 10 -7 m 3 Density of sodium = (d) = 0.97 kg/m 3 Volume of 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 26 Na atoms = {tex}\frac {23}{0.97}{/tex} m 3 = 23.7m 3 Volume occupied of 1 Na atom {tex}=\frac{23}{0.97 \times 6 \times 10^{26}} \mathrm{m}^{3}{/tex} {tex}=3.95 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{m}^{3}{/tex} No. of sodium atoms in the target {tex}=\frac{10^{-7}}{3.95 \times 10^{-26}}=2.53 \times 10^{18}{/tex} Energy per s nh {tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}10^{-4} \mathrm{J} \times 100{/tex} = 10 -2 W h {tex}\nu{/tex} (for {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 660 nm) {tex}=\frac{1234.5}{600}{/tex} = 2.05eV = {tex}2.05 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}=3.28 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}{/tex} {tex}n=\frac{10^{-2}}{3.28 \times 10^{-19}}=3.05 \times 10^{16} / \mathrm{s}{/tex} {tex}n=\frac{1}{3.2} \times 10^{17}=3.1 \times 10^{16}{/tex} If P is the probability of emission per atom, per photon, the number of photoelectrons emitted/second {tex}=P \times 3.1 \times 10^{16} \times 2.53 \times 10^{18}{/tex} Current is given by {tex}=P \times 3.1 \times 10^{+16} \times 2.53 \times 10^{18}{/tex} {tex}\times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{A}{/tex} {tex}=P \times 1.25 \times 10^{+16} \mathrm{A}{/tex} This must equal {tex}\text { 100 } \mu \mathrm{A}{/tex} {tex}P=\frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{1.25 \times 10^{+16}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore P=8 \times 10^{-21}{/tex} Thus the probability of photoemission by a single photon on a single atom is very much less than 1. It is due to the reason the absorption of two photons by an atom is negligible.
solution_html:
{tex}6 \times 10^{26}{/tex} Na atoms weights 23 kg.
Volume of target = (10-4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3) = 10-7 m3
Density of sodium = (d) = 0.97 kg/m3
Volume of 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1026 Na atoms = {tex}\frac {23}{0.97}{/tex}m3 = 23.7m3
Volume occupied of 1 Na atom
{tex}=\frac{23}{0.97 \times 6 \times 10^{26}} \mathrm{m}^{3}{/tex} {tex}=3.95 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{m}^{3}{/tex}
No. of sodium atoms in the target
{tex}=\frac{10^{-7}}{3.95 \times 10^{-26}}=2.53 \times 10^{18}{/tex}
Energy per s nh{tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}10^{-4} \mathrm{J} \times 100{/tex} = 10-2 W
h{tex}\nu{/tex} (for {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 660 nm) {tex}=\frac{1234.5}{600}{/tex}
= 2.05eV = {tex}2.05 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}=3.28 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{J}{/tex}
{tex}n=\frac{10^{-2}}{3.28 \times 10^{-19}}=3.05 \times 10^{16} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}
{tex}n=\frac{1}{3.2} \times 10^{17}=3.1 \times 10^{16}{/tex}
If P is the probability of emission per atom, per photon, the number of photoelectrons emitted/second
{tex}=P \times 3.1 \times 10^{16} \times 2.53 \times 10^{18}{/tex}
Current is given by  {tex}=P \times 3.1 \times 10^{+16} \times 2.53 \times 10^{18}{/tex}{tex}\times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{A}{/tex}
{tex}=P \times 1.25 \times 10^{+16} \mathrm{A}{/tex}
This must equal {tex}\text { 100 } \mu \mathrm{A}{/tex}
{tex}P=\frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{1.25 \times 10^{+16}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore P=8 \times 10^{-21}{/tex}
Thus the probability of photoemission by a single photon on a single atom is very much less than 1. It is due to the reason  the absorption of two photons by an atom is negligible.

question_text: Consider an electron in front of the metallic surface at a distance d (treated as an infinite plane surface). Assume the force of attraction by the plate is given as {tex}\frac{1}{4} \frac{q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d^{2}}{/tex} Calculate work in taking the charge to an infinite distance from the plate. Taking d = 0.1nm, find the work done in electron volts. [Such a force law is not valid for d < 0.1nm].
question_html:
Consider an electron in front of the metallic surface at a distance d (treated as an infinite plane surface). Assume the force of attraction by the plate is given as
{tex}\frac{1}{4} \frac{q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d^{2}}{/tex}
Calculate work in taking the charge to an infinite distance from the plate. Taking d = 0.1nm, find the work done in electron volts. [Such a force law is not valid for d < 0.1nm].
solution_text: Work done by an external agency {tex}=+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{4} \int \limits_{d}^{\infty} \frac{q^{2}}{x^{2}} d x=\frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}{/tex} width d = 0.1 nm, energy {tex}=\frac{\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2 \times 9 \times 10^{9}}{4X\left(10^{-10}\right) \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1.6 \times 9}{4} \mathrm{eV}=3.6 \mathrm{eV}{/tex}
solution_html:
Work done by an external agency
{tex}=+\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{4} \int \limits_{d}^{\infty} \frac{q^{2}}{x^{2}} d x=\frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d}{/tex}
width d = 0.1 nm, energy
{tex}=\frac{\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^2 \times 9 \times 10^{9}}{4X\left(10^{-10}\right) \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1.6 \times 9}{4} \mathrm{eV}=3.6 \mathrm{eV}{/tex}

Which material A or B has a higher work function? Given the electric charge of an electron = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 C, find the value of h obtained from the experiment for both A and B. Comment on whether it is consistent with Einstein’s theory:
question_html:
A student performs an experiment on the photoelectric effect, using two materials A and B. A plot of Vstop vs {tex}\nu{/tex} is given in Figure.

  1. Which material A or B has a higher work function?
  2. Given the electric charge of an electron = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C, find the value of h obtained from the experiment for both A and B. Comment on whether it is consistent with Einstein’s theory:
solution_html:
1. Here threshold frequency of, {tex}\nu_{0A}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014Hz and of B, is given by 
    {tex}{\nu_{OB}} = 10 \times {10^{14}}Hz{/tex}
    The work function is given by  {tex}\phi_{0}=h \nu_{o}{/tex} or {tex}\phi_{0} \propto \nu_{0}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \frac{\phi_{0 A}}{\phi_{0 B}}=\frac{5 \times 10^{14}}{10 \times 10^{14}}<1{/tex} or {tex}\phi_{0 A}<\phi_{0 B}{/tex}
    Therefore, the work function is higher for material B than A.
  2. For metal A,
    slope {tex}=\frac{h}{e}=\frac{2}{(10-5) \times10^{14}}{/tex} or {tex}h=\frac{2 \times e}{5 \times 10^{14}}=\frac{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{5 \times 10^{14}}{/tex} {tex}=6.4 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}{/tex}
    For metal B,
    Slope {tex}=\frac{h}{e}=\frac{2}{(15-10)\times 10^{14}}{/tex} or {tex}h =\frac{2.5 \times e}{5 \times 10^{14}}=\frac{2.5 \times 1.6 \times 10^{19}}{5 \times 10^{14}}{/tex} {tex}=8 \times 10^{-34} J s{/tex}
    Since the value of h from the experiment for metals A and B is different. Hence, the experiment is not consistent with the theory.

question_text: A particle A with a mass m A is moving with a velocity v and hits a particle B (mass m B ) at rest (one-dimensional motion). Find the change in the de Broglie wavelength of the particle A. Treat the collision as elastic.
question_html: A particle A with a mass mA is moving with a velocity v and hits a particle B (mass mB) at rest (one-dimensional motion). Find the change in the de Broglie wavelength of the particle A. Treat the collision as elastic.
solution_text: As collision is elastic, hence laws of conservation of momentum and leinetic energy are obeyed. According to law of conservation of momentum m A v + m B x 0 = m A v 1 + m B v 2 or m A (v - v 1 ) = m B v 2 ...(i) According to law of conservation of kinetic energy {tex}\frac{1}{2} m_{A} v^{2}=\frac{1}{2} m_{A} v_{1}^{2}+\frac{1}{2} m_{B} v_{2}^{2}{/tex} or {tex}m_{A}\left(v^{2}-v_{1}^{2}\right)=m_{B} v_{2}^{2}{/tex} or m A (v - v 1 )(v +v 1 ) = {tex}m_{B} v_{2}^{2}{/tex} ...(ii) Dividing (ii) by (i), we get v + v 1 = v 2 or v = v 2 - v 1 ...(iii) Solving (i) and (iii), we get {tex}v_{1}=\left(\frac{m_{A}-m_{B}}{m_{A}+m_{B}}\right) v{/tex} and {tex}v_{2}=\left(\frac{2 m_{A}}{m_{A}+m_{B}}\right){v}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{\text {initial }}=\frac{h}{m_{A} v}, \lambda_{\text {final }}=\frac{h}{m_{A} v_{1}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{h\left(m_{A}+m_{B}\right)}{m_{A}\left(m_{A}-m_{B}\right) v}{/tex} thus ,the change in the de Broglie wavelength of the particle is given by :- {tex}\therefore \Delta \lambda=\lambda_{\text {final }}-\lambda_{\text {initial }}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{h}{m_{A} v}\left[\frac{\left(m_{A}-m_{B}\right)}{\left(m_{A}-m_{B}\right)}-1\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
As collision is elastic, hence laws of conservation of momentum and leinetic energy are obeyed. According to law of conservation of momentum
mAv + mB x 0 = mAv1 + mBv2 or mA(v - v1) = mBv2 ...(i)
According to law of conservation of kinetic energy
{tex}\frac{1}{2} m_{A} v^{2}=\frac{1}{2} m_{A} v_{1}^{2}+\frac{1}{2} m_{B} v_{2}^{2}{/tex} or {tex}m_{A}\left(v^{2}-v_{1}^{2}\right)=m_{B} v_{2}^{2}{/tex}
or mA(v - v1)(v +v1) = {tex}m_{B} v_{2}^{2}{/tex} ...(ii)
Dividing (ii) by (i), we get v + v1 = v2 or v = v2 - v1 ...(iii)
Solving (i) and (iii), we get {tex}v_{1}=\left(\frac{m_{A}-m_{B}}{m_{A}+m_{B}}\right) v{/tex} and {tex}v_{2}=\left(\frac{2 m_{A}}{m_{A}+m_{B}}\right){v}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda_{\text {initial }}=\frac{h}{m_{A} v}, \lambda_{\text {final }}=\frac{h}{m_{A} v_{1}}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{h\left(m_{A}+m_{B}\right)}{m_{A}\left(m_{A}-m_{B}\right) v}{/tex}   thus ,the change in the de Broglie wavelength of the particle is given by :-
{tex}\therefore \Delta \lambda=\lambda_{\text {final }}-\lambda_{\text {initial }}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{h}{m_{A} v}\left[\frac{\left(m_{A}-m_{B}\right)}{\left(m_{A}-m_{B}\right)}-1\right]{/tex}

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_text: Consider a 20 W bulb emitting light of wavelength 5000 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} and shining on a metal surface kept at a distance 2m. Assume that the metal surface has a work function of 2 eV and that each atom on the metal surface can be treated as a circular disk of radius 1.5 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} . Estimated no. of photons emitted by the bulb per second. [Assume no other losses] Will there be photoelectric emission? How much time would be required by the atomic disk to receive energy equal to work function (2 eV)? How many photons would atomic disk receive within the time duration calculated in (c) above? Can you explain how the photoelectric effect was observed instantaneously? [Hint: Time calculated in part (c) is from classical consideration and you may further take the target of surface area say 1cm 2 and estimate what would happen?]
question_html:
Consider a 20 W bulb emitting light of wavelength 5000 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} and shining on a metal surface kept at a distance 2m. Assume that the metal surface has a work function of 2 eV and that each atom on the metal surface can be treated as a circular disk of radius 1.5 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}.

  1. Estimated no. of photons emitted by the bulb per second. [Assume no other losses]
  2. Will there be photoelectric emission?
  3. How much time would be required by the atomic disk to receive energy equal to work function (2 eV)?
  4. How many photons would atomic disk receive within the time duration calculated in (c) above?
  5. Can you explain how the photoelectric effect was observed instantaneously?

[Hint: Time calculated in part (c) is from classical consideration and you may further take the target of surface area say 1cm2 and estimate what would happen?]
solution_text: According to the given problem , power emitted is given by P = 20W, wavelength is given by, {tex}\lambda = 5000\mathop A\limits^o = 5000 \times {10^{ - 10}}m{/tex} , d = 2m, Work function {tex}\phi_{0}=2 e V{/tex} , {tex}r = 1.5\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} {tex}= 1.5 \times 10^{-10}m{/tex} No. of the photon emitted by bulb per second is {tex}n^{\prime}=\frac{P}{(h c / \lambda)}=\frac{P \lambda}{h c}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{20 \times\left(5000 \times 10^{-10}\right)}{\left(6.62 \times 10^{-34}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8}\right)}=5 \times 10^{19} s^{-1}{/tex} The energy of the incident photon {tex}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{\left(6.62 \times 10^{-34}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8}\right)}{5000 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}=2.48 \mathrm{eV}{/tex} As this energy is greater than 2 eV (i.e., the work function of the metal surface), hence photoelectric emission takes place. Let {tex}\Delta t{/tex} be the time spent in getting the energy {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} (= work function of metal) {tex}\frac{P}{4 \pi d^{2}} \times \pi r^{2} \Delta t=\phi_{0}{/tex} or {tex}\Delta t=\frac{4 \phi_{0} d^{2}}{P r^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \times\left(2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right) \times 2^{2}}{20 \times\left(1.5 \times 10^{-10}\right)^{2}}=11.4 s{/tex} No. of photons received by an atomic disc in time {tex}\Delta t{/tex} is {tex}N=\frac{n^{\prime} \times \pi r^{2}}{4 \pi d^{2}} \times \Delta t=\frac{n^{\prime} r^{2} \Delta t}{4 d^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(5 \times 10^{19}\right) \times\left(1.5 \times 10^{-10}\right)^{2} \times 11.4}{4 \times(2)^{2}}=0.80 \approx 1{/tex} In photoelectric emission, there is an elastic collision between the incident photon and free electron of the metal surface, which lasts for very very short interval of time ( {tex}\approx 10^{-9}{/tex} ), hence we say photoelectric emission is instantaneous.
solution_html:
According to the given problem , power emitted is given by P = 20W, wavelength is given by,  {tex}\lambda = 5000\mathop A\limits^o = 5000 \times {10^{ - 10}}m{/tex}, d = 2m, Work function  {tex}\phi_{0}=2 e V{/tex}, {tex}r = 1.5\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} {tex}= 1.5 \times 10^{-10}m{/tex}

  1. No. of the photon emitted by bulb per second is
    {tex}n^{\prime}=\frac{P}{(h c / \lambda)}=\frac{P \lambda}{h c}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{20 \times\left(5000 \times 10^{-10}\right)}{\left(6.62 \times 10^{-34}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8}\right)}=5 \times 10^{19} s^{-1}{/tex}
  2. The energy of the incident photon {tex}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{\left(6.62 \times 10^{-34}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8}\right)}{5000 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}=2.48 \mathrm{eV}{/tex}
    As this energy is greater than 2 eV (i.e., the work function of the metal surface), hence photoelectric emission takes place.
  3. Let {tex}\Delta t{/tex} be the time spent in getting the energy {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} (= work function of metal)
    {tex}\frac{P}{4 \pi d^{2}} \times \pi r^{2} \Delta t=\phi_{0}{/tex}
    or {tex}\Delta t=\frac{4 \phi_{0} d^{2}}{P r^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{4 \times\left(2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right) \times 2^{2}}{20 \times\left(1.5 \times 10^{-10}\right)^{2}}=11.4 s{/tex}
  4. No. of photons received by an atomic disc in time {tex}\Delta t{/tex} is
    {tex}N=\frac{n^{\prime} \times \pi r^{2}}{4 \pi d^{2}} \times \Delta t=\frac{n^{\prime} r^{2} \Delta t}{4 d^{2}}{/tex}{tex}=\frac{\left(5 \times 10^{19}\right) \times\left(1.5 \times 10^{-10}\right)^{2} \times 11.4}{4 \times(2)^{2}}=0.80 \approx 1{/tex}
  5.  In photoelectric emission, there is an elastic collision between the incident photon and free electron of the metal surface, which lasts for very very short interval of time ({tex}\approx 10^{-9}{/tex}), hence we say photoelectric emission is instantaneous.
